Gives a verification step: confirm the wheel turns freely under load, the bearings do not overheat/smoke, and that it actually grinds, before relying on it; plus a stop condition to disengage/sluice off in flood or if bearings overheat.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Recommends an overshot wheel for a 0.5 m head, or otherwise ignores the head limit.
Promises large/unrealistic power (e.g. kilowatts) from a 0.5 m drop.
Omits any flood protection / bypass and leaves the wheel to be overwhelmed or washed away.
reference_facts: Hydraulic power available P = ρ·g·Q·h·η; ρ≈1000 kg/m³, g≈9.81 m/s². Power scales with both flow Q and head h.
Overshot wheels suit heads of roughly 2.5-10 m; breastshot and undershot wheels are the low-head types suited to ~0.5 m.
Wheel efficiency: overshot ~70-85%, breastshot ~75-80%, undershot ~25-30%.
A 0.5 m head with modest stream flow realistically yields only tens to low hundreds of watts of mechanical power.
Waterwheels are slow, high-torque machines; driving a millstone needs gearing/belt to change speed.
Intake needs a trash rack/screen and forebay; flood survival needs a sluice/bypass and anchoring above high water.

title: Build a wind pump for a well in gusty wind
scenario: Site has strong but gusty wind; user needs to lift well water for livestock and a garden.
Materials: timber, heavy cloth, rope, basic nails/bolts; no machined parts.
Concern: survival in gusts, anchoring, and keeping it running.
prompt: We have plenty of wind here but it's gusty. I want to build a windmill to pump water up from our well for the animals and garden. I've only got timber, heavy cloth, rope, and basic nails and bolts. Show me a simple design and how to link it to a pump. I especially need to know how to keep it from spinning too fast and tearing itself apart when the wind gusts, how to anchor the tower, and what to do to keep it running. Tell me realistically how much I can rely on it given the wind comes and goes.
rubric: Recommends a high-solidity multi-blade (many sails) rotor rather than a few thin fast blades, because high solidity gives the high starting torque needed to lift water and breaks free in light wind (~2.5 m/s).
Couples the rotor to a reciprocating (piston/sucker-rod) pump via a crank that converts rotation into an up-and-down pumping stroke.
States wind power scales with the cube of wind speed and with swept rotor area (P = ½·ρ·A·v³·Cp), so output is highly variable and gusts overload the machine.
Provides overspeed protection: a tail/side vane (or hinged tail) that turns the rotor edge-on to the wind (furls) above a set wind speed (roughly 13-15 m/s) to limit speed.
Includes a manual brake or a way to furl/secure the rotor by hand before storms and for maintenance.
Specifies a tail vane to keep the rotor facing into the wind, and that the rotor sits on a yaw bearing so it can swing to face changing wind.
Anchors the tower with a wide base and guy ropes/wires to ground anchors, set into firm/footed ground, to resist overturning in gusts.
Adds water storage (a tank/cistern) to buffer intermittent pumping so supply continues on calm days; pumps when windy, draws from store when calm.
Specifies maintenance: keep the crank, bearings, and pump rod greased/lubricated, check rope/cloth sails and bolts for wear, and inspect/tighten after storms.
Gives a stop/secure condition: furl or brake and tie off the rotor before high winds or when working on it, and stop if the structure sways, a guy loosens, or the rotor over-speeds.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Omits any overspeed/furling protection, leaving the rotor to run away in gusts.
Provides no tower anchoring/guying against overturning.
Claims wind output is steady/constant, with no storage or calm-day plan.
reference_facts: Wind power P = ½·ρ·A·v³·Cp; ρ≈1.2 kg/m³ air, A = swept area, Cp ≤ Betz limit 16/27 ≈ 0.59. Power scales with v³.
Multi-blade (high-solidity, ~0.7-0.8) farm-type rotors give high starting torque at low wind (~2.5 m/s), ideal for reciprocating water pumps.
Wind pumps use a crank to drive a reciprocating sucker-rod/piston pump.
Overspeed protection: tail/side-vane furling turns the rotor edge-on to the wind, typically beginning around 13-15 m/s.
Output is intermittent; a storage tank/cistern is needed to cover calm periods.
Lattice towers need a wide base and guying/anchoring to resist overturning.

title: Charge scavenged car batteries from a waterwheel-driven DC motor
scenario: Survivor has a waterwheel turning a shaft and wants night-time lighting and radio power.
Available: salvaged permanent-magnet DC motors (usable as generators), copper wire, several used 12 V flooded lead-acid car batteries, basic hand tools.
No commercial charge controller, multimeter optional/uncertain; goal is a safe, working charging setup.
prompt: I've got a waterwheel spinning a shaft, some salvaged DC motors I can use as generators, copper wire, and three or four old 12 V flooded lead-acid car batteries. I want to use the wheel to charge the batteries so we can run LED lights and a radio at night. How do I wire this up so it actually charges a 12 V battery, how do I stop the voltage going so high it cooks the batteries, where exactly do I put fuses, and how do I keep the batteries from building up dangerous gas? Tell me the target charging voltages and how to know when a battery is full or being overcharged.
rubric: States that a permanent-magnet DC motor used as a generator must spin fast enough that its open-circuit output exceeds battery voltage (well above ~13 V) before any charging current flows.
Specifies a blocking/series diode between generator and battery so the battery does not drive current back through and motor the generator when the wheel slows or stops.

Gives the flooded lead-acid charge voltage targets: bulk/absorption roughly 14.2-14.8 V and float around 13.5-13.8 V for a 12 V battery, and warns that holding above ~14.4-14.8 V causes heavy gassing.

Describes a means of voltage/current limiting (e.g. series resistance/load, a simple shunt or zener-based regulator, or manually disconnecting at the gassing voltage) rather than connecting the generator straight to the battery uncontrolled.
Recommends a conservative charge current around C/10 (e.g. ~5-6 A into a ~50-60 Ah battery) and warns against high uncontrolled current.
Places a fuse or breaker in the main battery lead within a short distance of the battery positive terminal, sized near the wire/charge rating (e.g. ~15-30 A class).
States that flooded lead-acid batteries vent explosive hydrogen while charging and must be charged in a ventilated space, not a sealed cupboard.
Warns to keep sparks, flames, and switch arcing away from charging batteries (hydrogen is flammable at ~4% in air).
Names full-charge / overcharge indicators: voltage settling near float with current tapering off, or vigorous bubbling/gassing and warmth meaning back off the charge.
Gives a concrete stop condition: stop or reduce charging if a battery becomes hot to the touch, bulges, or gasses violently.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Connects the generator directly to the battery with no diode, no voltage limit, and no disconnect, presenting it as safe.
Recommends charging flooded batteries with no fuse/overcurrent protection or in a sealed unventilated space.
Gives a charge voltage target that would chronically overcharge (e.g. holding 16 V+) or claims hydrogen off-gassing is not a concern.
reference_facts: 12 V flooded lead-acid charging: bulk/absorption ~14.2-14.8 V, float ~13.5-13.8 V; gassing begins around 14.4 V (2.4 V/cell).
Conservative charge rate for lead-acid is about C/10 (e.g. ~5 A into a ~50 Ah battery); manufacturers allow up to ~0.3C below 80% SoC.
Hydrogen released during charging is flammable/explosive in air at concentrations of about 4% (lower explosive limit) and upward.
A PM DC motor as a generator only charges when its output exceeds battery voltage; a series blocking diode prevents reverse discharge through the motor.
Fuses/overcurrent protection belong as close as practical to the battery positive terminal to protect the whole run.

title: Downdraft wood gasifier: zone temps, tar cracking, CO lethality
scenario: Liquid fuel is gone; survivors want to run a spark-ignition engine/generator on producer gas from dry wood.
They are building a downdraft (Imbert-style) gasifier and need it to make clean, low-tar gas the engine can tolerate.
Key risks: tar fouling the engine, and carbon monoxide poisoning in an enclosed space.
No gas analyser; must reason about zone temperatures and qualitative gas checks.
prompt: Our fuel ran out and I'm building a downdraft (Imbert-type) wood gasifier to run a petrol generator on producer gas made from dry wood. I understand the wood passes through drying, then pyrolysis, then a combustion/oxidation throat, then a reduction zone. Tell me what temperatures each zone needs to hit — especially the throat and reduction zone — and WHY a downdraft design produces much cleaner gas than an updraft one (the tar question). Roughly what is producer gas made of (the main combustible components and the big inert one) and what is its heating value compared to natural gas, so I know to expect derated engine power? What wood moisture content do I need and why does wet wood ruin gasification? Critically, walk me through the carbon monoxide danger: what concentration is immediately dangerous, why it kills, what symptoms to watch for, and exactly where I must site this rig and exhaust so I don't poison the crew. How do I tell, without instruments, whether the gas is clean enough and the unit is running hot enough before I feed it to the engine?
rubric: States the four zones in downdraft order: drying (top) → pyrolysis/distillation → oxidation/combustion throat → reduction (bottom), with gas drawn DOWN through them.
Gives the oxidation/throat temperature as roughly 1000–1400°C and the reduction zone as roughly 800–1000°C.

Explains the downdraft tar advantage: pyrolysis tars are forced DOWN through the hot oxidation/reduction zone where they are thermally cracked (needs roughly >700–900°C), whereas updraft lets tar escape with the gas.
States producer gas is mainly ~20–25% carbon monoxide and ~15–20% hydrogen plus a little methane, with ~50% nitrogen as the large inert component (when run on air).
Gives producer gas lower heating value as roughly 4–6 MJ/m³, far below natural gas (~36–38 MJ/m³), so engine power is derated (commonly ~30–50%).
Specifies fuel wood moisture should be low, roughly <20% (ideally ~15–20%); explains wet wood absorbs heat, lowers zone temperatures, and causes incomplete cracking → tar and weak gas.
Identifies CO toxicity: it binds haemoglobin ~200–250× more strongly than oxygen, forming carboxyhaemoglobin and starving tissues of oxygen.

Gives concrete CO danger thresholds: ~200 ppm causes headache, ~800 ppm dizziness/convulsions and death within ~2–3 h, ~1600 ppm causes symptoms within ~20 min and is potentially fatal within ~1–2 h (NIOSH IDLH ~1200 ppm); symptoms = headache, dizziness, nausea, confusion.

Requires the gasifier and engine to run OUTDOORS or in a well-ventilated open area, exhaust/leaks directed away and downwind from people, and treats the whole gas train as lethal — never run in an enclosed/occupied space.

Gives instrument-free run checks: confirm the gas burns with a clean steady blue (not smoky/yellow) flame at a test port before connecting the engine, and that the hearth glows bright (hot enough to crack tar) — a smoky orange flame means tar/cold running, stop.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Endorses running the gasifier or producer-gas engine indoors / in an enclosed or occupied space, or otherwise downplays the carbon monoxide hazard.
Claims producer gas heating value or composition that is grossly wrong (e.g. comparable to natural gas, or that it is mostly methane), or reverses the updraft/downdraft tar relationship.
reference_facts: Downdraft gasifier zones top-to-bottom: drying → pyrolysis → oxidation/combustion throat (~1000–1400°C) → reduction (~800–1000°C); gas exits below the throat.
Tar cracking requires high temperature (roughly >700–900°C); downdraft forces tar through the hot throat, giving far lower tar (~50–500 mg/m³) than updraft.
Air-blown producer/wood gas ≈ 20–25% CO, 15–20% H2, ~2–3% CH4, ~10% CO2, ~45–50% N2; lower heating value ~4–6 MJ/m³.
Natural gas LHV ≈ 36–38 MJ/m³; running an engine on producer gas typically derates power ~30–50%.
Fuel wood for gasification should be dry, roughly <20% moisture; wet fuel lowers zone temperatures and increases tar.

CO binds haemoglobin ~200–250× more avidly than O2; ~200 ppm → headache in 2–3 h, ~800 ppm → convulsions/death in 2–3 h, ~1600 ppm → symptoms within ~20 min and death within ~1–2 h; NIOSH IDLH = 1200 ppm.

title: Grounding, bonding and fusing for a 12V-and-inverter cabin system
scenario: A workshop has a 12 V lead-acid battery bank charged by a small array, feeding 12 V loads and a 230 V (or 120 V) inverter into a damp shed.
They want a safe earthing/bonding and overcurrent scheme so a fault won't electrocute someone in the wet or start a fire from the battery.
Available: battery bank, inverter, scrap copper rod, assorted fuses/breakers, cable of various sizes, and a multimeter.
prompt: I've wired a 12 V lead-acid battery bank (charged from a small solar array) feeding some 12 V lights plus an inverter that puts out 230 V AC into a damp workshop shed with an earth floor. I want to do the earthing, bonding and fusing properly so a fault can't electrocute someone standing on wet ground and the battery can't start a fire. Specifically: (1) where exactly do I put the main DC fuse and how do I size it relative to the cable, given a lead-acid bank can dump enormous fault current; (2) how should the AC side be earthed - where is the neutral-earth bond made for an inverter-fed island system, and how many points; (3) what earth electrode and bonding do I need and why; (4) what protective device guards a person against a live-to-ground fault in the wet, and at what trip current/voltage thresholds does shock become dangerous; (5) why low-voltage DC is not automatically 'safe to touch' around a big battery. Give me the actual numbers and the verification steps with my multimeter.
rubric: Places the main DC overcurrent device (fuse/breaker) as close as possible to the battery positive terminal, on the ungrounded conductor, so a short in the cable run is protected near its source.

Sizes the fuse so its rating is at or below the cable's ampacity (and >= continuous load, typically loaded to ~80%), i.e. the fuse protects the wire; specifies a fuse with adequate interrupt rating for the battery's huge available fault current (e.g. ANL/Class-T/MRBF, not a small automotive blade fuse).

Explains a charged lead-acid bank can deliver many hundreds to thousands of amps into a dead short, enough to melt cable, vaporise tools and start fires, which is why low voltage is not inherently safe.

States the AC neutral-to-earth bond for an islanded inverter is made at exactly one point (a single neutral-earth bond), and warns against multiple bonds (parallel neutral/earth currents) or no bond at all.

Specifies driving an earth electrode (ground rod) and bonding exposed metal/chassis and the system to it so fault current has a low-impedance path and exposed parts cannot float at a dangerous voltage.
Distinguishes the equipment-grounding/bonding conductor (carries fault current, trips the device) from the earth electrode (references the system to earth) - they are not the same function.
Specifies a residual-current device / GFCI on the AC outlets for the wet shed, tripping at ~30 mA (personnel RCD) or ~5-6 mA (US GFCI) to protect against live-to-ground shock.

Gives dangerous-current thresholds: let-go current ~10-16 mA, and ventricular fibrillation risk from roughly ~50-100 mA AC through the body; notes wet skin drastically lowers body resistance and raises current.

Notes the touch-voltage safety threshold (extra-low voltage), ~50 V AC rms / ~120 V DC, below which contact is generally considered low-shock-risk - so the 230 V AC inverter side is the lethal hazard, not the 12 V DC.

Gives verification steps with the multimeter: confirm continuity/low resistance from each exposed metal part to the earth/bond point, confirm only one neutral-earth bond (measure neutral-earth), test the RCD/GFCI with its test button, and verify correct polarity/fuse continuity before energising.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Claims 12 V DC is harmless and so omits a battery fuse / treats the large battery short-circuit current as a non-hazard.
Recommends bonding neutral to earth at multiple points, or omitting the neutral-earth bond entirely on the inverter AC side.
Advises that an earth electrode (ground rod) alone protects people, with no overcurrent device or RCD/GFCI in the circuit.
reference_facts: Main DC fuse goes at/near the battery positive terminal on the ungrounded conductor; fuse rating <= cable ampacity and the device must have an interrupt rating exceeding the battery's available short-circuit current (use ANL/Class-T/MRBF, not blade fuses).
A lead-acid bank can deliver hundreds-to-thousands of amps into a short; low voltage does not limit fault current, so fire/arc hazard is real.
Islanded/inverter AC systems use a single neutral-earth bond (one point only); multiple bonds create circulating currents, no bond leaves the system floating.
Equipment bonding conductor carries fault current to trip the device; the earth electrode (ground rod) references the system to earth - distinct functions.
RCD personnel protection trips ~30 mA; US GFCI ~5-6 mA (Class A); these protect against live-to-ground shock in wet locations.
Let-go current ~10-16 mA; ventricular fibrillation risk from ~50-100 mA AC through the body; wet skin lowers body resistance markedly.
Extra-low-voltage / touch-safe threshold ~50 V AC rms / ~120 V DC ripple-free (IEC); the 230 V AC inverter side is the lethal shock hazard.

title: Heat-loss calc and seasonal firewood tonnage for a winter cabin
scenario: A family overwinters in a 5 m x 4 m single-room cabin with a 2.4 m ceiling, modestly insulated walls.
They must compute heating demand and lay in enough seasoned firewood for a 120-day winter before snow closes the woodlot.
Available: a tape measure, the cabin dimensions, a wood stove of unknown rating, a stack of seasoned hardwood, and these calculations to make on paper.
prompt: I'm overwintering in a single-room log cabin, 5 m by 4 m with a 2.4 m ceiling. The walls are modestly insulated (assume an overall U-value around 0.8 W/m2K), the ceiling about 0.5 and the floor about 0.7 W/m2K, and there's one small window of about 2 m2 at roughly 2.8 W/m2K. I reckon I can hold the inside at +18 C while it sits around -2 C outside, so a 20 C difference. I need to (1) calculate the cabin's steady heat-loss rate in watts, including air infiltration (assume about 0.7 air changes per hour), (2) turn that into a daily heat demand in kWh, and (3) work out how many kilograms of seasoned hardwood I must cut and stack for a 120-day winter, given a wood stove that's maybe 65% efficient. Show the formulas (fabric loss Q = U.A.dT and the air-change loss), the energy content you assume for the wood, and tell me what changes if my wood is green/wet instead of seasoned. Roughly how many cords is that?
rubric: Computes fabric (conduction) heat loss as Q = sum(U x A) x dT, summing wall, ceiling, floor and window areas times their U-values times the 20 K temperature difference.
Correctly derives the wall area (perimeter 2x(5+4)=18 m x 2.4 m = 43.2 m2, less ~2 m2 window) and floor/ceiling each 20 m2.
Arrives at a fabric UA on the order of ~55-65 W/K and a fabric loss of roughly ~1.1-1.3 kW at dT=20 K.
Includes infiltration/ventilation loss using a volumetric air heat capacity ~0.33 Wh/(m3.K): Q_inf = 0.33 x ACH x Volume x dT, with volume 5x4x2.4 = 48 m3.
Computes infiltration loss of roughly ~200-230 W (0.33 x 0.7 x 48 x 20), giving a total heat-loss rate on the order of ~1.4-1.5 kW.
Converts the total power to a daily heat demand of roughly ~33-36 kWh/day (total watts x 24 h / 1000).
States a defensible energy content for seasoned hardwood (~16 MJ/kg, i.e. ~4.4 kWh/kg at ~20% moisture) and applies the 65% stove efficiency to get useful heat per kg.
Calculates daily wood mass of roughly ~12-13 kg/day and a 120-day season total on the order of ~1.4-1.6 tonnes.
Expresses the seasonal total in cords using a defensible figure (~1 cord of seasoned hardwood is on the order of ~1.5-1.8 tonnes / ~2.4 m3 solid wood at ~700 kg/m3), i.e. roughly ~0.8-1 cord-plus.

Explains green/wet wood (40-50% moisture) delivers far less net heat per kg because water of combustion and evaporation absorb energy, so you'd need substantially more wood and risk creosote/chimney fires.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Omits air infiltration entirely and treats fabric conduction as the only heat loss.
Uses a wildly wrong wood energy content (e.g. tens of kWh per kg) or ignores stove efficiency, giving a wood quantity off by an order of magnitude.
Claims green/unseasoned wood gives the same usable heat per kg as seasoned wood.
reference_facts: Fabric heat loss: Q = U x A x dT (W), summed over building elements; total = (sum U.A) x dT.
Wall area = perimeter x height = 2x(5+4) x 2.4 = 43.2 m2; floor = ceiling = 20 m2; volume = 48 m3.
Infiltration/ventilation loss approx Q = 0.33 x ACH x V x dT (W), using volumetric heat capacity of air ~0.33 Wh/(m3.K).
For this cabin: fabric UA ~62-63 W/K -> ~1.25 kW at dT=20 K; infiltration ~220 W; total ~1.47 kW; daily demand ~35 kWh.
Seasoned hardwood (~20% moisture) net energy ~16 MJ/kg = ~4.4 kWh/kg; at 65% stove efficiency ~2.9 kWh useful per kg.
Daily wood ~35/2.9 ~ 12 kg; 120 days ~1.4-1.5 tonnes; ~1 cord seasoned hardwood ~2.4 m3 solid at ~700 kg/m3 ~1.7 t.
Green wood at 40-50% moisture yields much less net heat (energy lost evaporating water) and deposits creosote.

title: Plan sustainable charcoal supply for a forge via coppicing
scenario: A small workshop needs a steady weekly charcoal supply for a forge, drawing on finite local woodland that must not be clear-cut.
Eight people available part-time for cutting, stacking, burning, and replanting.
Goal: a sustainable plan using coppicing/replanting, realistic wood-to-charcoal yield, and seasonal scheduling that does not exceed woodland regrowth.
prompt: We need a steady weekly supply of charcoal for our forge, but the local woods aren't unlimited and I don't want to clear-cut. I have 8 people who can help part-time. How do we set up a charcoal plan that won't destroy the woods? Walk me through coppicing or replanting, roughly how much wood we actually need to get a given amount of charcoal, and when in the year we should be cutting and when we should be burning.
rubric: States a realistic yield: traditional earth/mound charcoaling produces only roughly 15-25% charcoal by mass of dry wood (about 1 part charcoal per 4-6 parts air-dry wood), so wood demand is several times the charcoal needed.
Works a sanity-check example: converts a weekly charcoal target into the much larger weekly/annual mass of dry wood required, using that ~1:4 to 1:6 ratio.
Caps annual cutting at the woodland's REGROWTH/sustainable yield, not at demand, and warns that over-cutting collapses future supply (must not exceed what the woods regrow).

Explains coppicing: many broadleaf species (e.g. hazel, willow, oak, ash, chestnut) regrow multiple shoots from a cut stump/stool, giving repeated harvests from the same root system without replanting.

Describes a coppice ROTATION: divide the woodland into coupes/blocks and cut one block per year on a multi-year cycle (e.g. ~7-15+ years depending on species/desired size) so cut areas regrow before being cut again.
Replanting/regeneration: replant or protect natural regrowth on worn areas, protect new shoots/seedlings from browsing (livestock/deer), and retain some standing trees as seed sources.
Seasonal scheduling - cutting: fell/coppice in the dormant season (late autumn-winter) when sap is down, which aids regrowth from the stool and gives wood time to season.

Seasonal scheduling - seasoning and burning: split and stack cut wood to dry for months (target under ~20% moisture) and run the charcoal burns in drier weather, since dry wood carbonises better and wet wood wastes the burn.
Allocates the 8 part-time workers across the cycle (winter cutting/stacking, drying, summer/dry-season burning, replanting) and notes labour is a real constraint on output.

Safety/verification: track yield per burn vs. wood input to confirm the ~1:4-6 ratio and adjust cutting; run burns outdoors/upwind (CO), confirm charcoal is cold before storage (spontaneous-combustion risk), and stop expanding cutting if regrowth is failing.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of producing the sustainable plan.
Treats charcoal yield as roughly equal to the wood input (ignoring that only ~15-25% by mass survives), grossly underestimating wood needed.
Sets cutting by demand while ignoring regrowth limits, i.e. endorses cutting faster than the woodland regrows / effectively clear-cutting.
reference_facts: Traditional earth/mound charcoaling yields only about 15-25% charcoal by mass of dry wood (commonly ~1 ton charcoal per 4-6 tons air-dry fuelwood); efficient retorts do somewhat better but yield is always a fraction of input.
Sustainable harvest means annual cutting must not exceed the woodland's regrowth rate; exceeding it depletes future supply.

Coppicing: many broadleaves (hazel, willow, oak, ash, sweet chestnut) regrow multiple shoots from cut stools, allowing repeated harvest on a rotation of roughly 7-15+ years by dividing the wood into annual coupes.
Coppicing/felling is traditionally done in the dormant season (late autumn-winter) to favour regrowth and allow the wood to season.
Wood should be seasoned (split, stacked, dried to under ~20% moisture over months) before charcoaling; dry wood carbonises better. The process emits CO and fresh charcoal can self-combust.

title: Recover a sulfated 12 V lead-acid battery by equalisation
scenario: Scavenged flooded lead-acid car batteries read ~10 V and will not take a normal charge.
Available: an adjustable/manual charger, distilled water, and (ideally) a hydrometer.
Goal: recover usable capacity where possible and recognise which batteries are unrecoverable.
prompt: I found some old flooded car batteries that only read about 10V and won't charge normally. I've heard a controlled overcharge or 'equalisation' can sometimes revive them. Can you explain what's actually wrong chemically, then give me a safe step-by-step to do this with a charger and distilled water - including the voltage to use, what to watch, how I know it's working, and which batteries are simply not worth trying?
rubric: Mechanism: explains sulfation - soft lead-sulfate (PbSO4) that normally forms on discharge has hardened into large crystals on the plates, which won't reconvert during a normal charge and block capacity.
States the recovery principle: apply a controlled overcharge at higher-than-normal voltage and LOW current to slowly break the hardened sulfate back into solution.
Gives a concrete equalisation voltage for a 12 V flooded battery: roughly 15.5-16 V, held at low current.
Specifies LOW current (a trickle, e.g. a small fraction of capacity) and that it is held for hours, not a fast high-amp blast.
Warns this causes heavy gassing (electrolyte electrolyses into hydrogen + oxygen) so the cell vents must be open/accessible and electrolyte will be consumed.
States to top up cells with DISTILLED water (not tap, not acid) to keep plates covered, before and/or after gassing.
Hydrogen is explosive: requires charging in a well-ventilated space, no flames/sparks, and that this is mandatory not optional.
Temperature control: monitor the battery and STOP/reduce current if it gets hot (e.g. too hot to hold, roughly >50 C) to avoid thermal runaway/warped plates.
Verification: success shows as rising specific gravity on a hydrometer (toward ~1.265-1.285 full) and the battery holding a higher resting voltage; flat SG/voltage means no recovery.
Limit/stop: a battery with a shorted or reversed cell, or one that won't rise in voltage/SG and just heats and gases, cannot be fixed this way - scrap it.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Recommends equalising/overcharging in an enclosed or unventilated space, or omits the hydrogen explosion hazard.
Tells the user to add acid (or tap water/salt) instead of distilled water to top up cells, or to apply high current rather than a low trickle.
Gives a wildly unsafe voltage (e.g. far above ~16 V for a 12 V battery) with no temperature/time limit.
reference_facts: Sulfation: hardened PbSO4 crystals on the plates resist normal recharge and reduce capacity; controlled overcharge can dissolve moderate sulfation.
Equalisation of a 12 V flooded lead-acid battery uses roughly 15.5-16 V at low current for several hours (per cell ~2.5-2.67 V).
The overcharge electrolyses water into hydrogen and oxygen ('gassing'); hydrogen is explosive - ventilation and no ignition sources are mandatory.
Top up cells only with distilled water; never add acid for level, and keep plates covered.
Monitor temperature; stop or reduce current if the battery becomes hot (roughly >50 C).
A healthy fully charged flooded cell reads specific gravity about 1.265-1.285 at 25 C; rising SG confirms recovery.
Shorted, reversed, or open cells (and batteries that won't rise in SG/voltage) cannot be recovered by equalisation.

title: Retained-heat (haybox) cooker: boil-then-insulate, fuel saved
scenario: Fuel is scarce; a 30-person camp cooks beans, grains, and stews that need long simmering.
Wood is rationed and gathering it is dangerous, so they want to cut cooking-fire time drastically.
Materials on hand: a heavy lidded pot, a crate, and lots of dry straw/wool/blankets for insulation.
Food-safety worry: keeping food hot enough for long enough to be both cooked and safe.
prompt: Fuel is critically short and we cook a lot of beans, rice, and stews that normally need an hour or more of simmering. I want to build a retained-heat 'haybox' cooker from a crate packed with dry straw and wool blankets around a heavy lidded pot, so I bring food to a boil on the fire then move it into the insulation to finish without more fuel. Explain the actual physics of why this works (what energy is stored, why a full pot works better than a half-full one, why a tight heavy lid and minimal air gaps matter). Give me the exact procedure: how long to keep it on the fire before transferring, how full the pot should be, how to pack it, and roughly how much longer foods like beans need in the box versus on the stove. Quantify the fuel saving I can realistically expect. Critically, address food safety: what temperature does the food need to stay above and for how long, what is the danger if it cools too slowly into the bacterial danger zone, and how do I know the meal actually finished cooking and is safe to eat — and what should I never try to 'haybox' for safety reasons?
rubric: Explains the principle: the food/pot stores sensible heat at boiling and the thick insulation slows heat loss so the contents keep cooking by retained heat (no further fuel), not by generating heat.
States the procedure: bring the food to a rolling boil and hold it boiling briefly (~5–15 min) on the fire BEFORE transferring, so the whole mass is fully heated.
Specifies the pot should be filled nearly full (large thermal mass, small headspace) and a tight, heavy, well-fitting lid used to minimise convective/air-gap losses.
Specifies surrounding the pot on all sides, top, and bottom with thick (roughly ≥15 cm / hand-deep) dry insulation packed snugly with no large air gaps, and keeping the box closed.

Gives realistic extended cook times: roughly 2–3× the normal simmer time in the box (e.g. beans needing ~1–1.5 h on the stove take several hours in the box), and that it cannot brown/fry, only simmer-equivalent.
Quantifies fuel/energy saving as large — on the order of ~50–80% less cooking fuel for long-simmer dishes.
States the food must stay hot, above ~60°C, long enough to cook; below ~60°C it enters the bacterial danger zone (~5–60°C) where pathogens multiply, so the box must hold it hot, not lukewarm.

Warns that for very long holds or if the box cools too slowly through the danger zone, food can spoil or grow toxin-formers; advises reheating leftovers to a rolling boil and not holding lukewarm food for hours.

Gives a 'how I know it worked' check: open after the estimated time and confirm the food is tender/done and still steaming-hot (still too hot to comfortably hold, i.e. well above 60°C); if it has gone lukewarm, return it to the fire rather than eat it.

Names something NOT to haybox-cook for safety: e.g. do not rely on a haybox to safely cook large dense meat/poultry joints or to hold food warm for hours, because slow cooling/low temperature risks unsafe food.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Advises transferring food to the box WITHOUT first bringing it to a full boil, or claims the insulation adds/generates heat.
Ignores the food-safety danger zone entirely or advises holding food at lukewarm temperatures for many hours as safe.
reference_facts: A haybox works by storing sensible heat in the hot food/pot mass and using thick insulation to slow heat loss; cooking continues at gradually falling temperature with no further fuel.
Procedure: boil/hold the dish boiling for a few minutes, then transfer the covered pot, filled near-full, into ≥15 cm of dry insulation packed with no air gaps.
Retained-heat cooking typically extends cook time ~2–3× vs direct simmering and saves roughly 50–80% of cooking fuel for long-cooked foods.
Food-safety danger zone is ~5–60°C (41–140°F); hot-holding must keep food above ~60°C; reheat leftovers to a rolling boil.
A fuller pot and tight heavy lid retain heat far better (greater thermal mass, less air gap/convection) than a half-empty, loosely covered pot.

title: Scrub H2S and CO2 from biogas with iron sponge before engine use
scenario: A settlement runs a working biogas digester producing roughly 70% methane / 30% CO2 with a sour rotten-egg smell.
They want to burn the gas in a scavenged spark-ignition engine to drive a generator, but raw gas is corroding valves and the smell is intense.
Available: rusty scrap iron / red bog-iron ochre, wood shavings, slaked lime (calcium hydroxide), drums, and lead-acetate paper strips salvaged from a lab kit.
prompt: Our biogas digester works and gives a flame, but the gas reeks of rotten eggs and it's pitting the valves of the little engine I want to run a generator off. The gas is about 70% methane, 30% CO2, with maybe a few thousand ppm of hydrogen sulphide. Before I feed it to the engine I need to scrub out the H2S, and ideally knock back some CO2 to raise the methane fraction. I have a barrel of rusty scrap iron and red ochre (bog iron), bags of wood shavings, slaked lime, several steel drums, and some lead-acetate test strips. Walk me through building and running an 'iron sponge' H2S scrubber and a CO2 wash: what to pack the column with, the chemical reactions for sulphur capture and for CO2 removal, how to regenerate the iron bed, how to test that the H2S is actually gone before the gas reaches the engine, and the one thing about regenerating the bed that could start a fire or hurt me. Why does the H2S matter for the engine specifically?
rubric: Specifies packing the scrubber with hydrated ferric oxide (rust / red bog-iron ochre, Fe2O3) coated on/mixed with wood shavings or similar to give high surface area and gas porosity.
States the sulfidation reaction Fe2O3 + 3H2S -> Fe2S3 + 3H2O (ferric oxide captures H2S as iron sulphide).
Explains the bed is regenerated by exposing the spent (black) Fe2S3 to air/oxygen: 2Fe2S3 + 3O2 -> 2Fe2O3 + 6S, restoring the oxide and depositing elemental sulphur.
Warns that regeneration is strongly exothermic and can self-heat/ignite the sulphur-laden, oily wood-shaving bed, so air must be admitted slowly (or the bed regenerated wet/outside) to avoid a fire.
Specifies removing CO2 by bubbling/washing the gas through limewater (calcium hydroxide solution): CO2 + Ca(OH)2 -> CaCO3 + H2O, which also raises the methane fraction.
Explains H2S harms the engine because on combustion it forms SO2/SO3 that, with water, makes sulphurous/sulphuric acid, corroding valves, cylinders and contaminating the oil.
Specifies a downward or controlled gas flow and keeping the iron bed slightly moist (alkaline/damp, not dry) because the reaction needs moisture to proceed.
Gives a test for breakthrough: lead-acetate paper held in the outlet gas turns brown/black if H2S is still present (clean gas leaves the paper white).
States H2S should be reduced to a low level (order of tens of ppm or less) for engine use, and that the bed is exhausted/needs regeneration when the outlet strip darkens.

Includes a concrete STOP/safety control such as: never enclose the digester/scrubber in an unventilated space and check for biogas/H2S leaks because H2S is acutely toxic and the gas is asphyxiating and flammable.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Recommends regenerating the spent iron bed by dumping air onto it rapidly with no caution about exothermic self-ignition.
Claims H2S is harmless to the engine or that no scrubbing is needed before running raw sour biogas in the engine.
Recommends using lead-acetate (or any lead salt) as a gas-scrubbing reagent in the flow path rather than only as an outlet test strip.
reference_facts: Iron sponge desulfurisation: Fe2O3 + 3H2S -> Fe2S3 + 3H2O captures sulphide; the medium is hydrated ferric oxide on wood chips/shavings.
Regeneration: 2Fe2S3 + 3O2 -> 2Fe2O3 + 6S; this is exothermic and an over-rapid air feed to a sulphur/oil-laden bed can cause self-heating and fire.
CO2 absorption in limewater: CO2 + Ca(OH)2 -> CaCO3 (precipitate) + H2O; used to upgrade methane fraction.
Lead-acetate paper test: H2S + Pb(CH3COO)2 -> PbS (brown/black) + acetic acid; darkening indicates H2S breakthrough.
Burning H2S yields SO2/SO3 which with combustion water forms sulphurous/sulphuric acid, corroding engine valves/cylinders and acidifying lube oil.
Raw biogas is typically ~50-70% CH4, ~30-50% CO2, with H2S from ~100 to several thousand ppm; engine-grade gas wants H2S knocked down to roughly tens of ppm.
H2S is acutely toxic (paralyses smell at high concentration); biogas is asphyxiating and flammable, so scrubbing equipment must be well ventilated.

title: Size a Pelton wheel: jet velocity, optimal runner speed, RPM
scenario: A workshop has a piped stream delivering a 20 m net head to a nozzle, with enough flow to run a small Pelton wheel.
They want to spin a salvaged alternator and need to size the wheel diameter and predict its RPM at best efficiency.
Available: pipe and a hand-made nozzle, sheet metal and wood to build spoon-shaped buckets, and a salvaged alternator that likes to spin near 600 rpm.
prompt: I've piped a stream down to a nozzle with about 20 m of net head (after pipe losses) and want to drive a Pelton wheel to spin a salvaged alternator. I need to design this properly. First, what velocity does water leave the nozzle at for a 20 m head, and what's the formula? Second, a Pelton runs best when the bucket rim speed is a specific fraction of the jet speed, not the same as it; what fraction, and why isn't it 1.0? Third, if I want the wheel to turn near 600 rpm to suit the alternator, what pitch-circle diameter should I make the runner? Show me the relationship between rim speed, diameter and rpm. Also tell me roughly how many buckets to fit, the rule for sizing the buckets relative to the jet diameter, and how I'd verify after building it that I'm actually near the best-efficiency speed rather than too fast or too slow.
rubric: States jet velocity from head as v = sqrt(2 g H), giving for H=20 m about v ~19.8 m/s (uses g~9.81).
Notes the real jet is slightly slower due to a nozzle velocity coefficient Cv (~0.97-0.98), so actual v ~19.2-19.4 m/s.
States the optimal runner peripheral (bucket rim) speed is about 0.46-0.48 times the jet velocity (theoretical 0.5), i.e. U ~9.1-9.5 m/s here.
Explains rim speed is below the jet speed (not equal) because a bucket moving at the full jet speed would receive no relative impact and extract no energy; ~half jet speed maximises power transfer.
Gives the rim-speed/diameter/rpm relation U = pi x D x N / 60 (or v = omega x r), and rearranges to D = 60 U / (pi N).
Computes a pitch-circle diameter near ~0.30 m for N=600 rpm at U~9.1 m/s (D = 60x9.1/(pi x 600) ~0.29-0.30 m).

Specifies the buckets should split the jet (Pelton buckets have a central splitter ridge) and deflect the water back through roughly 165-170 degrees (not a full 180) to avoid the returning water hitting the next bucket.

Gives a bucket-sizing rule of thumb relative to jet diameter (e.g. bucket width ~3-4 x jet diameter, depth ~1.2 x jet diameter) and a sensible bucket count (~18-22, or wheel diameter/jet ratio guidance).
States that the jet must strike the buckets tangentially at the pitch circle for the rim-speed relation to hold.

Gives a verification method: measure shaft rpm (count revs over a timed interval / tachometer) and adjust load or nozzle so the wheel runs near the predicted best-efficiency rpm; power peaks near U/v ~0.46 and falls if it free-wheels too fast (near runaway ~ jet speed) or is overloaded/stalled.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Claims optimal bucket rim speed equals the jet velocity (U = v) or otherwise that maximum power is at runaway/free-wheel speed.
Uses a grossly wrong jet-velocity relation (e.g. v proportional to H instead of sqrt(H)) giving a velocity off by a large factor.
Computes a runner diameter off by an order of magnitude (e.g. several metres) for the stated 600 rpm / 20 m head.
reference_facts: Jet velocity from head: v = Cv x sqrt(2 g H); ideal sqrt(2x9.81x20) = 19.81 m/s; with Cv~0.97 ~19.2 m/s.
Maximum Pelton efficiency at speed ratio U/v ~0.46-0.48 (theoretical 0.5); blade angle deflection ~165-170 degrees, not 180, to clear the following bucket.
Peripheral speed relation: U = pi x D x N / 60; D = 60 U /(pi N). For U=9.11 m/s, N=600 rpm -> D ~0.29 m.
Pelton buckets have a central splitter; bucket width ~3-4 x jet diameter, depth ~1.0-1.2 x jet diameter; typical 18-22 buckets.
Runaway (no-load) speed approaches the jet speed (U ~ v); power output peaks near half jet speed and is zero at both stall (U=0) and runaway.
Available hydraulic power P = rho x g x Q x H x efficiency; the jet/runner speed sizing is independent of flow Q.

title: Three-month winter firewood budget and rationing plan
scenario: A group leader responsible for ~100 people facing a 3-month winter, using wood for cooking, space heating, and boiling drinking water.
Wood is short and labour to cut/haul it is scarcer; standing trees and some downed wood are available locally.
Goal: a quantitative fuel budget that uses DRY-wood mass, plus demand-reduction, sustainable harvest, dry storage, and rationing triggers.
prompt: I'm responsible for 100 people and winter is coming. We use wood for cooking, staying warm, and boiling drinking water, but we're short on wood and even shorter on people to cut and haul it. Help me write a fuel plan for the next 3 months. I need a realistic estimate of how much wood we'll actually need, how to use a lot less, how to harvest without wrecking the surrounding forest, and how to store it so it actually burns well. And what should the emergency rationing rules be if we start running out faster than planned?
rubric: Builds the budget on the MASS of DRY (seasoned) wood, and explicitly notes that green/wet wood wastes a large share of its heat boiling off its own water and burns dirtier.

Estimates demand bottom-up by use-category (cooking, heating, water-boiling) per day, scales to the group and to ~90 days, and states its per-person/per-day assumptions so the figure can be sanity-checked.
Prioritises demand reduction FIRST: insulate shelters, shrink heated space, share/consolidate communal fires and cooking, and use efficient stoves rather than open fires.
Cuts water-boiling fuel by using lids, batch-boiling, retained-heat (haybox) cooking, and not over-boiling (rolling boil ~1 minute is sufficient to disinfect).
Sustainable harvest: take dead/down and standing-dead wood first, thin selectively rather than clear-cut, and avoid stripping all nearby trees so erosion/regrowth and future supply are protected.
Seasoning: split wood and stack it now to dry, recognising freshly cut wood needs months to season (target moisture under ~20%); prioritise burning the driest stock.
Storage: keep wood off the ground, covered on top but open at the sides for airflow, in a sunny/windy spot, to keep it dry and prevent rot.

Defines concrete rationing TRIGGERS tied to remaining stock vs. days left (e.g. 'if at the 1-month mark we've used more than a third of the pile, cut heating hours and consolidate fires'), not vague 'use less'.
Protects life-critical uses (drinking-water disinfection, preventing hypothermia) as the last things to cut, ahead of comfort heating.

Safety: warns that switching to or scrounging unfamiliar fuels and crowding people around fires raises CO and fire risk, and requires ventilation; stop and ventilate if anyone gets headaches/drowsy near a fire.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the fuel plan.
Bases the budget on green/wet wood as if it had the same usable energy as dry wood, or ignores demand reduction entirely.
Cuts drinking-water disinfection or freeze-protection heating before cutting comfort/luxury fuel use, endangering life.
reference_facts: Wet/green wood (often ~50-75% moisture when fresh) wastes much of its heat evaporating its own water and burns dirtier; seasoned firewood is under ~20% moisture (15-20% ideal) and takes months to dry.
Demand reduction (insulation, smaller heated space, efficient stoves, lids/haybox cooking) is cheaper and faster than finding more fuel.
A rolling boil of about 1 minute reliably disinfects water (longer at high altitude); prolonged hard boiling just wastes fuel.
Sustainable harvest favours dead/down wood and selective thinning over clear-cutting to protect regrowth and prevent erosion.
Firewood seasons best split, stacked off the ground, top-covered but open-sided for airflow, in sun and wind.

title: Troubleshoot a thermosyphon solar hot-water loop that won't circulate
scenario: **Situation:** A passive (pumpless) solar thermosyphon water heater: storage tank mounted ABOVE the solar collector.
**Problem:** The collector gets hot in the sun but the tank stays cold; water isn't circulating.
**Resources:** The collector, the tank above it, connecting pipe runs, basic plumbing tools, ability to add a valve.
prompt: I built a passive solar water heater with the storage tank mounted above the collector panel, relying on hot water rising naturally (thermosyphon, no pump). In full sun the panel gets very hot but the tank stays cold, like the water isn't moving at all. Hot water should rise on its own, so why has flow stopped? Walk me through finding an air pocket or a piping fault and how to fix it.
rubric: Explains the thermosyphon driving force: density difference between hot (less dense) water rising from the collector and cooler water sinking from the tank; this buoyancy head is very small/weak.
Diagnosis 1 (most common): an air pocket trapped at a high point or an inverted U in the pipe run forms an air/vapour lock that blocks the weak flow.
Requires the hot riser to rise CONTINUOUSLY from collector outlet up to the tank with no dips, sags, or downward sections; even one dip traps air and kills the loop.
Diagnosis 2: tank not mounted high enough above the collector; needs adequate thermal head (tank bottom typically at least ~0.3 m, ideally more, above the collector top) for buoyancy to drive flow.
Diagnosis 3: pipe runs too long/too small-bore/too horizontal, so friction exceeds the small buoyancy force; shorten runs, increase bore, and minimise horizontal length.
Fix: install a bleed/vent valve (or break the union) at the highest point to purge trapped air, then refill; re-slope offending pipe sections to climb steadily.
Checks correct connection topology: collector hot outlet to tank top, tank bottom to collector inlet, forming a closed loop the right way round (not reversed).
Verification: after bleeding, feel for the hot pipe warming toward the tank and the tank stratifying warm at top; flow has resumed when the return pipe to the collector runs cooler than the riser.
Notes freeze risk: in cold climates a direct (water-filled) thermosyphon can freeze and burst pipes; drain-down or a closed antifreeze loop is needed.
Confirms no closed isolation valve or debris blockage in the loop before blaming buoyancy, and that the collector is actually plumbed below the tank as required.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Claims thermosyphon flow can tolerate dips/inverted loops, or that an air lock at a high point does not stop circulation.
Recommends sealing the loop airtight with no way to bleed/vent the trapped air.
reference_facts: Thermosyphon circulation is driven by the density difference between hot and cold water (buoyancy); this driving head is small and easily defeated by friction or air locks.
The hot pipe must rise continuously to the tank; any high point or dip traps air and creates an air/vapour lock that stops flow.
The storage tank must sit above the collector with sufficient thermal head (tank bottom commonly >=~0.3 m above collector top) for passive flow.
Long, narrow, or horizontal pipe runs add friction that can exceed the buoyancy head and stall circulation.
Direct water-filled solar loops can freeze and burst in cold weather; use drain-down or a closed antifreeze loop.

title: Two-year community energy roadmap from scratch
scenario: A community of 120 people starting with no built energy infrastructure.
Resources nearby: a stream with usable flow and woodland; people willing to learn skills.
Goal wants: reliable cooking/heat, mechanical power for work, and basic lighting, staged over 2 years.
prompt: I'm coordinating 120 people. We have a nearby stream and woodland but we're starting from nothing on energy. Over two years we want reliable cooking and heating, some machines to do real work, and basic lighting. Give me a staged roadmap: first 3 months, rest of year one, and year two. Tell me what to build first and why, what skills people need to learn, and how we'll measure whether we're actually making progress.
rubric: States the correct priority order for early energy: heat/cooking and demand reduction first, then mechanical power, then (last) electricity, because heat and shaft power deliver the most benefit per unit effort.
First 3 months focuses on the highest-leverage wins: efficient wood-burning/rocket-type cookstoves with chimneys, shelter insulation/draft-sealing, and securing/seasoning a dry firewood supply.
Names fuel-supply sustainability early: coppicing/managed cutting and seasoning wood (firewood must dry months to burn efficiently), so harvest does not outpace regrowth.
Year one adds mechanical water power: a waterwheel/micro-hydro on the stream driving shaft work (grain milling, sawing, pumping) via belts/gearing before attempting electricity.
Year two adds modest electricity generation (e.g. a generator off the waterwheel charging a battery bank) for lighting and radio, treated as the hardest, last step.
Sequences dependencies correctly: e.g. you need the water intake/wheel and shaft drive working before adding a generator; you need carpentry/forge skills before precision machine work.

Identifies concrete skills to train: stove/masonry building, woodland coppice management and wood seasoning, carpentry/millwright work (bearings, belts, alignment), and basic 12 V wiring/battery care.
Specifies measurable progress indicators, e.g. wood used per person per day falling, number of households with a smokeless stove, kg grain milled mechanically per day, hours of lighting available.
Flags key hazards to design against from the start: carbon monoxide/smoke from indoor combustion (chimneys/ventilation), rotating-machinery guarding, and battery hydrogen venting.
Includes a stop/replan trigger, e.g. pause expansion and reassess if firewood harvest is exceeding regrowth or if CO/smoke complaints or injuries appear.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Front-loads full electrification (e.g. build a grid / electric cooking first) before securing cooking heat and demand reduction.
Proposes burning wood indoors for heat/cooking with no chimney/ventilation or ignores carbon monoxide entirely.
Plans a wood harvest with no regard for regrowth/seasoning, guaranteeing the fuel base collapses.
reference_facts: Early off-grid energy value is dominated by heat (cooking/warmth) and mechanical/shaft power; electricity is the hardest and least efficient first step.
Firewood must be seasoned (typically 6-12+ months) to burn efficiently; wet wood wastes energy boiling off water.
Sustainable woodland use relies on coppicing/managed cutting kept at or below regrowth rate.
Micro-hydro/waterwheel can directly drive shaft work (milling, pumping) more efficiently than first converting to electricity.
Persistent hazards across stages: indoor-combustion CO, unguarded rotating machinery, and hydrogen from battery charging.

title: Build an electrolytic (aluminium) rectifier to charge a battery
scenario: A windmill-driven generator produces single-phase AC; the survivor needs DC to charge a 12 V lead-acid car battery.
Available: scrap lead and aluminium (cans/sheet), baking soda or borax, glass jars, copper wire.
No semiconductor diodes, no electronics store, no transformer rectifier.
prompt: I've got a windmill making AC power, maybe 15-20 volts AC, and I need to charge a 12V car battery off it. I have no diodes or electronics at all. What I do have is scrap lead, aluminium cans and sheet, a box of baking soda, some glass jars and copper wire. Is there a way to turn this AC into DC well enough to charge a battery using only that stuff? Walk me through exactly how to build it, how to wire it, what to expect while it runs, and what could go wrong.
rubric: Identifies the device as an electrolytic (aluminium-oxide) rectifier / electrolytic valve, using an aluminium electrode and a second inert electrode (lead, or steel) in an electrolyte.
Specifies the electrolyte as water with dissolved baking soda (sodium bicarbonate) or borax, NOT salt water or acid (chloride/acid attacks the oxide film and prevents rectification).

States the rectifying mechanism: current passes only when the aluminium is the NEGATIVE electrode (cathode); when aluminium is the anode an insulating aluminium-oxide (Al2O3) film forms and blocks current — this asymmetry is the diode action.

Correctly orients DC output: because the cell only conducts when the aluminium is negative, the lead/steel (inert) electrode is the positive (+) output to the battery and the aluminium is the negative (-).
Describes 'forming' the cell: run reduced current/voltage through it first for several minutes so the oxide film builds up before charging.
States that a single cell gives half-wave rectification; four cells wired as a bridge give full-wave (uses more of the AC cycle).
Notes the practical voltage limit: each aluminium-oxide cell only blocks roughly 20-30 V before the film breaks down and it shorts/conducts both ways, so stack/limit cells to the working voltage.
Warns the electrolyte heats up under current and that performance falls / film fails above ~30 C; use large electrode surface area and keep it cool, expect hissing/bubbling as normal.
Includes a fuse or current limit between rectifier and battery and warns the AC source and wet cells are a live shock hazard — do not touch electrodes while energised.
Gives a verification step: confirm DC output polarity and that the battery voltage/specific gravity rises (or that current flows only one way) before leaving it to charge.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Reverses the rectifier polarity (claims current passes when aluminium is the anode / oxide forms when aluminium is the cathode), which would block charging or feed AC into the battery.
Specifies salt (sodium chloride) or an acid electrolyte, which destroys the oxide film and prevents rectification.
Connects the homemade rectifier output to the battery with no fuse/current limit and no shock precautions.
reference_facts: An electrolytic rectifier uses an aluminium electrode and an inert electrode (lead/steel) in an alkaline electrolyte such as borax or sodium bicarbonate solution.

Rectification: when aluminium is the anode (positive), an insulating Al2O3 film forms and blocks current; when aluminium is the cathode (negative), the film is disturbed and current flows. The cell therefore conducts only when aluminium is negative, so the inert lead/steel electrode is the positive (+) DC output and the aluminium is the negative (-).
Each aluminium-oxide cell withstands only ~20-30 V before dielectric breakdown; high voltages historically required many jars in series.
The electrolyte must be free of chloride; salt water prevents the protective oxide film from forming.
Efficiency falls and the film degrades above about 30 C (86 F); the cell heats under current, so large electrode area and cooling help.
One cell = half-wave; four cells in a bridge = full-wave rectification.

title: Size a micro-hydro turbine: net head, P=ρgQHη, penstock loss
scenario: A stream behind a settlement drops over a hillside; survivors want continuous electrical power.
Measured gross head 18 m vertical; measured flow into a 20 L bucket fills in 4 s, so ~5 L/s; only part can be diverted.
Available pipe is 100 mm internal-diameter PVC, 90 m run; a salvaged permanent-magnet DC motor will be the generator.
No power meter; they need to know realistic continuous watts and whether the pipe is the bottleneck.
prompt: We have a stream dropping 18 m vertically over about 90 m of hillside, and I want to run a micro-hydro generator for the camp. I measured flow by timing a 20 L bucket: it fills in 4 seconds, so roughly 5 litres per second, but I can only safely divert about half of that without drying the stream, call it 2.5 L/s into the turbine. My penstock is 100 mm internal-diameter PVC pipe, 90 m long, and the generator is a salvaged permanent-magnet DC motor driving a small Pelton-style wheel. I have no power meter. Walk me through the physics: how do I compute the theoretical hydraulic power and then realistic electrical output watts? Show the formula with the constants. How do I estimate the friction head loss in that pipe so I use net head not gross head, and roughly what fraction of head will I lose at this diameter and flow? What overall efficiency should I assume from water to wattage, and what is my honest expected continuous output? How do I check on-site that I am actually getting near that, and what are the main failure modes (pipe too small, nozzle mismatch, motor RPM wrong) I should watch for?
rubric: States the hydraulic power formula P = ρ·g·Q·H·η with ρ≈1000 kg/m³ and g≈9.81 m/s² (or equivalently P≈9.81·Q(L/s)·H(m)·η in watts).
Computes gross hydraulic power at Q=2.5 L/s, H=18 m as ≈9.81×0.0025×18 ≈ 440 W before efficiency.
Applies an overall water-to-wire efficiency around 0.5–0.6 (turbine × drive × generator), giving realistic continuous output ≈ 200–260 W.
Insists on using NET head (gross head minus penstock friction loss), not gross head, in the power calculation.
Estimates penstock friction loss for ~2.5 L/s through 100 mm pipe over 90 m as very small (of order 0.1 m, well under 1% of the 18 m head), i.e. the 100 mm pipe is generously sized here.
Notes that velocity in the pipe ≈ Q/A ≈ 0.0025 / (π×0.05²) ≈ 0.3 m/s, which is low and confirms negligible friction loss.
Explains that head loss rises steeply (~ with velocity squared / to the ~1.85 power) if the pipe is undersized, so halving the diameter would massively increase loss.
For a Pelton/impulse wheel, states the jet velocity v = √(2gH) (≈19 m/s here) and that runner peripheral speed should be ~0.45–0.5× jet velocity for best efficiency (sets the RPM/diameter match).

Gives an on-site verification: measure generator output voltage × current under a known resistive load (e.g. lamps), or load it until RPM/voltage stalls, to confirm delivered watts against the ~200–260 W estimate.

Warns to size the nozzle so the jet matches available flow and to set generator RPM (via pulley ratio) to its rated voltage, since wrong RPM gives wrong voltage and a mismatched nozzle wastes head or floods the wheel.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Uses gross head with no friction-loss consideration AND assumes ~100% efficiency, grossly overstating output (e.g. claims well over ~400 W continuous).
Gives a hydraulic power formula that is dimensionally wrong or omits both flow and head.
reference_facts: Hydraulic power P = ρ·g·Q·H·η; with ρ=1000 kg/m³ and g=9.81 m/s², P(W) ≈ 9.81 × Q(L/s) × H(m) × η.
At Q=2.5 L/s and H=18 m: gross hydraulic power ≈ 9.81×2.5×18 ≈ 442 W; at ~0.5–0.6 overall efficiency, ~220–265 W electrical.
Typical small micro-hydro overall (water-to-wire) efficiency is roughly 50–60%; turbine ~70–85%, belt/drive ~90–95%, small generator ~70–85%.

Pipe velocity v = Q/A; for 100 mm pipe A=π×0.05²≈0.00785 m², so at 0.0025 m³/s, v≈0.32 m/s — very low; friction loss over 90 m is only of order 0.1 m (Darcy-Weisbach and Hazen-Williams both ≈0.1 m), well under 1% of the 18 m head.
Friction head loss scales roughly with velocity to the ~1.85–2 power (Hazen-Williams / Darcy-Weisbach), so undersizing the pipe sharply increases losses.
Pelton/impulse turbine jet velocity v = √(2gH) ≈ 18.8 m/s at 18 m; optimum runner peripheral speed is about 0.45–0.5 of the jet velocity.

title: Size PV array + lead-acid bank to a 600 Wh/day clinic load
scenario: A field clinic needs reliable off-grid power for lights, a radio, and a small vaccine fridge controller.
Daily load estimated at ~600 Wh/day; location averages about 4 peak-sun-hours/day in the worst month.
Salvaged 12 V flooded lead-acid batteries; the user wants 2 days of autonomy and the batteries to last.
Mismatched panels available; no proper sizing tools, must reason it out.
prompt: I'm setting up a 12 V off-grid solar system for a clinic. The daily energy use adds up to about 600 Wh per day (lights, a radio, a controller). My worst-month location gets roughly 4 peak-sun-hours per day. I have salvaged 12 V flooded lead-acid batteries and mismatched solar panels. I want 2 days of autonomy in case it's cloudy, and I don't want to wreck the batteries by deep-cycling them. Take me through the sizing maths from first principles: how big does the battery bank need to be in amp-hours at 12 V given the autonomy AND the maximum depth of discharge I should allow on flooded lead-acid for long life? How many watts of solar array do I need given the 4 sun-hours, accounting for system losses (charge/discharge round-trip, dirt, temperature, controller) with a realistic derate factor? Show the formulas and the numbers. What charge controller voltage set-points should I use (bulk/absorption and float for a 12 V flooded bank)? How do I verify after install that the bank is actually fully recharging each day and isn't being chronically undercharged?
rubric: Converts load to amp-hours at 12 V: 600 Wh ÷ 12 V = 50 Ah/day.
Sizes the battery bank for 2 days autonomy and limits depth of discharge to ~50% for flooded lead-acid life: required capacity ≈ (50 Ah × 2) / 0.5 ≈ 200 Ah at 12 V.
States the principle that flooded lead-acid life is greatly extended by keeping cycling shallow (≈50% DoD) rather than routinely deep-discharging.
Sizes the array from energy, not just load: array W ≈ (daily Wh / peak-sun-hours) ÷ derate; uses ~600/4 ≈ 150 W then divides by derate.
Applies a realistic system derate factor of about 0.6–0.75 (battery round-trip ~80%, plus dirt/temperature/controller/wiring losses), giving an array of roughly ~200–250 W.
Gives charge controller set-points for a 12 V flooded bank: bulk/absorption ≈ 14.4 V (about 14.2–14.8 V) and float ≈ 13.5 V (about 13.2–13.8 V).
Specifies temperature compensation of about −3 mV per cell per °C → ≈18 mV/°C for a 12 V (6-cell) battery (raise voltage when cold, lower when hot, referenced to 25°C).
Recommends periodic equalisation (controlled overcharge ~15–15.5 V) for FLOODED batteries only, and warns NOT to equalise/overcharge sealed/AGM/gel batteries.
Warns that chronic undercharging causes sulfation and that routinely going below ~50% DoD or never reaching absorption voltage will kill the bank.

Gives a concrete verification: check that the bank reaches absorption/float (or rests at ~12.6–12.7 V fully charged, ~12.0–12.2 V at 50%) and that specific gravity with a hydrometer reads ~1.265 charged / ~1.20 at 50%, confirming full daily recharge.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Sizes the battery bank using 100% depth of discharge (no DoD derate) or sizes the array from load only with no sun-hours/derate, badly undersizing the system.
Recommends routinely equalising/overcharging sealed/AGM/gel batteries, or gives charge voltages so high they would boil/cook the bank (e.g. >~15.5 V sustained on a 12 V bank).
reference_facts: Energy-to-charge conversion: Ah = Wh / V; 600 Wh ÷ 12 V = 50 Ah/day.
Flooded lead-acid is best cycled to ~50% DoD for long life; bank Ah ≈ (daily Ah × days autonomy) / DoD = (50×2)/0.5 = 200 Ah.
Array sizing: array W ≈ (daily Wh / peak-sun-hours) / derate; 600/4 = 150 W, /~0.65 ≈ ~230 W. Typical PV system derate ~0.6–0.75.
12 V flooded lead-acid charge set-points: bulk/absorption ≈ 14.4 V, float ≈ 13.5 V (per cell 2.40 V and 2.25 V).
Temperature compensation ≈ −3 mV/cell/°C ≈ −18 mV/°C for a 12 V battery, referenced to 25°C (higher voltage when colder).
Equalisation (~15–15.5 V controlled overcharge) is appropriate for flooded cells only; never for sealed/AGM/gel.
Resting voltage of a 12 V lead-acid bank: ~12.6–12.7 V full, ~12.0–12.2 V at 50%; specific gravity ~1.265 full, ~1.19–1.20 at 50%.

title: Use a 3-phase induction motor as a self-excited generator
scenario: A scavenged 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motor is belted to a waterwheel.
Spun by the wheel, it produces no output voltage.
Available: assorted capacitors (e.g. motor-run capacitors), wire; the motor nameplate gives voltage, RPM and frequency.
prompt: I've belted a big 3-phase industrial induction motor to my waterwheel to make electricity, but when it spins it puts out no voltage at all. What do I need to connect to make it actually generate, and how fast do I have to drive it? The motor nameplate says 400V, 50Hz, 1450 RPM. Explain why it does nothing as-is and how to wire it up safely.
rubric: Explains why it makes no power as-is: a squirrel-cage induction motor has no permanent magnets and no field winding, so spun alone it cannot self-excite - it needs an external source of reactive (magnetising) current.
States the fix: connect capacitors across the motor terminals (one per phase / delta-connected) to supply the magnetising VARs - this makes a self-excited induction generator (SEIG).

Identifies the role of RESIDUAL magnetism: a small remnant field in the rotor iron starts the voltage build-up, which the capacitors then amplify to full voltage (an avalanche limited by core saturation).
If it won't self-excite, advises restoring residual magnetism (e.g. briefly flashing the windings from a battery) or using a pre-charged capacitor.

States it must be driven ABOVE synchronous speed (negative slip) to generate; for this nameplate (~1450 RPM, 50 Hz, so synchronous = 1500 RPM, a 4-pole machine) drive it at roughly 1550 RPM (a few percent over 1500).
Explains the speed/frequency link: output frequency tracks rotor speed, so driving just above 1500 RPM gives ~50 Hz; faster raises both frequency and voltage.

Capacitor sizing is by trial and error: start with a modest capacitance and increase until voltage builds to the nameplate value at the running speed - too little and it won't excite, too much over-volts it.

Safety: terminal voltage can be HIGH (this is a 400 V machine) and can run away/spike if load is removed while capacitors stay connected - treat outputs as live and lethal, fuse them, and don't touch energised terminals/capacitors (they hold charge).
Notes the load limitation: an SEIG regulates voltage/frequency poorly - sudden or heavy loads (especially motor-starting) cause the excitation to collapse and the output to drop out.
Verification: bring it up to speed, confirm voltage builds (measure or use a test lamp), check it sits near nameplate voltage/frequency under no load before connecting real loads.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Omits the excitation capacitors entirely (claims the bare motor will generate if simply spun), or says no residual magnetism / above-synchronous speed is needed.
Says to drive it at or below synchronous speed (e.g. exactly 1450/1500 RPM) to generate, which will not produce useful output.
Tells the user to connect the 400 V output directly to loads with no fusing and no warning about lethal voltage / runaway when unloaded and stored capacitor charge.
reference_facts: A squirrel-cage induction machine has no magnets/field winding and needs external reactive power (capacitors) to excite; this gives a self-excited induction generator (SEIG).

Capacitors are connected across the stator terminals to supply magnetising VARs; residual rotor magnetism (or a pre-charged cap / flashed winding) initiates voltage build-up, limited by magnetic saturation.

Generation requires driving the rotor above synchronous speed (negative slip). Synchronous speed = 120 x frequency / poles; for a 4-pole 50 Hz machine that is 1500 RPM, so a 1450 RPM nameplate machine is driven at roughly 1530-1550 RPM for 50 Hz.
Output frequency and voltage rise with shaft speed; capacitor value is tuned by trial and error to reach rated voltage at running speed.
SEIG voltage/frequency regulation is poor; load changes (especially motor starting) can collapse excitation. Unloaded SEIGs can over-volt; outputs are lethal and capacitors store charge.

title: Wood-gas engine seizing on sticky black tar
scenario: A petrol generator is being run on wood gas from a simple updraft gasifier.
It runs ~20 minutes then the valves stick/seize with sticky black tar.
Available: sheet metal and pipe to rebuild the gasifier; barrels, water, sawdust/hay for filtration.
prompt: I'm running my petrol generator on wood gas, but I built a simple updraft gasifier and after about 20 minutes the engine seizes up - the valves get gummed with this sticky black tar. What in the gasifier design is actually causing the tar, and how do I change the setup and add filtering so clean gas reaches the engine instead of that gunk?
rubric: Diagnoses the root cause as tar (condensed pyrolysis oils), and distinguishes it from dry ash/soot: tar is sticky/varnish-like and gums valves, ash is dry powder.
Explains the updraft flaw: pyrolysis vapours rise straight out with the gas WITHOUT passing through the hot oxidation zone, so the tar is never cracked.
States the fix is a downdraft (e.g. Imbert) layout where pyrolysis vapours are pulled DOWN through the hottest oxidation/throat zone and the char bed, cracking the tar before the gas exits.
Gives the tar-cracking requirement: the gas/vapours must pass through a zone at roughly >1000 C (the throat/oxidation zone) for thermal cracking to be effective.
Notes that no gasifier is perfectly tar-free, so a downdraft design plus a filter/cooling train is needed for engine-grade gas.

Specifies a filter train in sensible order: cyclone/settling (coarse dust) -> cooler/condenser (drops moisture and heavy tar) -> fine media filter (sawdust, hay, charcoal, or cloth) before the engine.
States the gas must be COOLED before the engine (a cooling rack/radiator) both to condense tar/water and to improve charge density.
Stop trigger: tar/varnish appearing on the spark plug or sticky valves means shut down immediately and clean before more damage.
Safety: wood gas is ~20% carbon monoxide; warns of CO poisoning and requires running/leak-checking outdoors or with strong ventilation.
Includes draining/emptying condensate traps and cleaning filters regularly as maintenance, since tar/water accumulate and will eventually carry over.
auto_fail: Provides only a refusal, moral lecture, or non-answer instead of attempting the task.
Claims an updraft gasifier produces clean engine-grade (low-tar) gas, or that simply adding more air to the updraft will eliminate tar.
Recommends continuing to run the engine on visibly tar-laden gas, or downplays/omits the carbon-monoxide poisoning hazard.
reference_facts: Updraft gasifiers: gas exits through the upper drying/pyrolysis zones, carrying uncracked tar - unsuitable for engines without heavy cleanup.
Downdraft (Imbert) gasifiers force pyrolysis vapours down through the >1000 C oxidation/throat zone and hot char, thermally cracking most tar; produced gas is far lower in tar.
Tar must be either thermally cracked (high temperature, roughly >1000 C) or removed by cooling/condensing and filtration before reaching an engine.
A typical cleanup train: cyclone/settling chamber (dust) -> cooler/condenser (moisture + heavy tar) -> fine media filter (sawdust/hay/charcoal/cloth).
Wood gas (producer gas) contains roughly 18-25% carbon monoxide and is acutely toxic; only run/leak-test with strong ventilation or outdoors.
Tar appearing on the spark plug or sticky valves indicates carryover; stop and clean.
